entities.name: Bulletin No. 141
entities.context: Board of Indian Commissioners bulletin dated May 4, 1931 regarding releasing Indians from supervision

events.description: First competency commission visited district, removed restrictions for many applicants who had shown business capacity
Edward P. Snead appeared before Investigation Committee for House Committee of Indian Affairs regarding extension of restrictions
Approach of 1931 when period of restriction will terminate for Five Civilized Tribes unless extended
Act of Congress removed restrictions on mixed blood Indians of Five Civilized Tribes
Oklahoma admitted into the Union as a state, vast changes observed in Indian living conditions
48 patents-in-fee issued to original allottees prior to 1916
91 patents-in-fee issued to original allottees since 1916
Fort Peck Superintendent built approximately fifty homes for Indians who sold portions of their land over four years
Mollie Johnson defrauded of land without fair consideration, loss $750
Daniel Johnson defrauded of land for practically nothing, loss $900
Adeline Grayson deluded into transferring allotment without consideration
Henderson Arbor defrauded out of land, loss $850
Approximately eighty allotments made to whites or near whites, mostly French-Canadian extraction, married or adopted into tribe
Superintendent allowed patents-in-fee to practically all young and middle aged people asking for it, putting up about fifty homes

financial_transactions.description: Sale of timber stumpage from allotments
Loss incurred by Mollie Johnson (Roll No. 9130) conveying 160 acres without fair consideration
Loss incurred by Daniel Johnson (Roll 9131) deeding away land for practically nothing
Loss incurred by Henderson Arbor (Roll No. 9613) defrauded out of land
Estimated total loss by 1300 mixed-bloods (75% defrauded of 70% value at $1040 per allotment)
Estimated total loss by 567 mixed-bloods (80% of 630 at $960 per allotment)
54 patents-in-fee lands lost through mortgages at Coeur d'Alene
Sale of land for automobiles, common practice when patent received
Sale of land for cattle or other stock
Appraised value per allotment in Hughes County for 160 acres
Appraised value per allotment in Seminole County

relationships.subject: Edward P. Snead
relationships.context: Field Clerk at Idabel, member of Choctaw tribe

relationships.subject: French-Canadians
relationships.context: Married into or adopted into tribe, received approximately eighty allotments

relationships.subject: White men
relationships.context: Women married to white men on Coeur d'Alene reservation, practically white class

relationships.subject: Edward P. Snead
relationships.context: Member of tribe representing their interests

fee_patents.context: Coeur d'Alene: 48 patents to original allottees prior to 1916
Coeur d'Alene: 91 patents to original allottees since 1916, 54 sold or lost through mortgages
Coeur d'Alene: 55 of 139 patents issued to whites/near whites (French-Canadians married into tribe)
Fort Peck: 204 patents for full allotments, 36 nothing left, 31 unknown disposal, 13 built homes, 56 purchased machinery/stock, 1 retained land, 1 cash in bank
Fort Peck: 333 patents for part of allotments (irrigable forties or quarter section grazing land)
One-quarter blood Creek, defrauded of land
One-quarter blood Creek, defrauded of land
One-half blood Creek, defrauded of land
One-quarter blood Creek, defrauded of land

correspondence.subject: Issuance of patents-in-fee, Indian competency, and reservation conditions
correspondence.action_requested: None specified, providing views on bulk citizenship and home building
correspondence.outcome: Report submitted with statistics on 537 patents issued

correspondence.subject: Patents-in-fee to Indian wards of Government
correspondence.action_requested: Consideration of views on granting patents only after self-supporting demonstration
correspondence.outcome: Views expressed against bulk citizenship, 139 patents statistics provided

correspondence.subject: Releasing Indians from Government Supervision
correspondence.action_requested: Reports and opinions on releasing Indians from supervision and extent of restrictions
correspondence.outcome: Bulletin No. 141 issued with collected letters from Oklahoma

correspondence.subject: Releasing Choctaw Indians from supervision
correspondence.action_requested: Extension of restriction period beyond 1931, protection for those unable to handle affairs
correspondence.outcome: Report submitted on 1917 commission visit and 1930 testimony

correspondence.subject: Releasing Creek and Seminole Indians from supervision
correspondence.action_requested: Adequate protection for those unable to handle affairs, repeal of laws facilitating fraud
correspondence.outcome: Report submitted with specific fraud cases and financial losses

legislative_actions.outcome: Removed restrictions on mixed blood Indians of Five Civilized Tribes
Extension of period of restriction entertained only in case of absolute necessity
Repeal of law providing restricted lands become unrestricted upon death of allottee
Repeal of law authorizing execution of valid wills disposing of restricted property without departmental approval
Allotment of old reservations, Indians made citizens
